---
title: "Foundations of machine learning"
subtitle: "Machine Learning in Finance · Session 4"
# Not self-contained: quarto-live's Pyodide runtime cannot resolve its worker
# from a data: URI, so embed-resources is off. Ship session_04.html together
# with session_04_files/ and data/, and SERVE OVER HTTP (double-click
# serve.cmd, or publish to a URL). The live code cells do not run from file://.
#
# Like Session 3 this deck loads numpy, pandas and matplotlib into the browser
# runtime (roughly 20 MB, once per page load). Open the deck and run one cell a
# few minutes before class so it is warm.
format:
  live-revealjs:
    theme: [default, ../theme/mlfin.scss]
    width: 1280
    height: 720
    margin: 0.06
    center: false
    transition: fade
    transition-speed: fast
    background-transition: none
    slide-number: c/t
    controls: false
    progress: true
    hash: true
    history: false
    code-line-numbers: false
    highlight-style: github
    auto-stretch: false
# KaTeX rather than the default MathJax: it renders the equations faster and
# without reflowing the slide. Like the Pyodide runtime it comes from a CDN, so
# this deck wants a connection either way.
html-math-method: katex
engine: jupyter
pyodide:
  packages:
    - numpy
    - pandas
    - matplotlib
  resources:
    - data/prices.csv
  cell-options:
    autorun: true
    startover: true
execute:
  echo: true
  warning: false
---

# Where three sessions of Python left you

```{python}
#| include: false
# House style for every precomputed figure in this deck.
import matplotlib as mpl
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

MLFIN_STYLE = {
    "figure.facecolor": "#fcfcfb", "axes.facecolor": "#fcfcfb",
    "savefig.facecolor": "#fcfcfb",
    "font.family": ["Segoe UI", "Arial", "sans-serif"],
    "font.size": 12, "text.color": "#141413", "axes.edgecolor": "#c3c2b7",
    "axes.labelcolor": "#52514e", "axes.titlecolor": "#141413",
    "axes.titlesize": 13, "axes.spines.top": False, "axes.spines.right": False,
    "axes.grid": True, "grid.color": "#e6e5df", "grid.linewidth": 0.8,
    "axes.axisbelow": True,
    "xtick.color": "#898781", "ytick.color": "#898781",
    "xtick.labelsize": 10.5, "ytick.labelsize": 10.5,
    "legend.frameon": False, "legend.fontsize": 10.5,
}
plt.rcParams.update(MLFIN_STYLE)

from pathlib import Path
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np

_data = Path("../data/prices.csv")
if not _data.exists():
    raise FileNotFoundError(
        "Missing ../data/prices.csv - clone the full course repository, or "
        "run tools/build_dataset.py from the repository root."
    )
PX = pd.read_csv(_data, parse_dates=["date"])
WIDE = PX.pivot(index="date", columns="ticker", values="close")
RET = WIDE.pct_change().dropna()

BLUE = "#1c5cab"
BLUE_LIGHT = "#86b6ef"
AMBER = "#b8860b"
BRICK = "#b3402f"
GREY = "#c3c2b7"
GREY_LIGHT = "#d8d7d0"
INK = "#141413"

# One invented dataset, used wherever the deck needs to draw a truth that real
# data never shows you. It comes back in Part 5 as the overfitting example.
def true_f(x):
    return np.sin(2 * np.pi * x)

_rng = np.random.default_rng(11)
SD = 0.28
XT = np.sort(_rng.uniform(0, 1, 30))
YT = true_f(XT) + _rng.normal(0, SD, 30)
XE = np.sort(_rng.uniform(0, 1, 400))
YE = true_f(XE) + _rng.normal(0, SD, 400)
XGRID = np.linspace(0, 1, 300)
```

```{pyodide}
#| include: false
# House style for the live figures, so they match the printed ones, plus the
# three imports every cell in this deck assumes.
import numpy as np
import pandas as pd
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
plt.rcParams.update({
    "figure.facecolor": "#fcfcfb", "axes.facecolor": "#fcfcfb",
    "font.family": ["Segoe UI", "Arial", "sans-serif"], "font.size": 12,
    "text.color": "#141413", "axes.edgecolor": "#c3c2b7",
    "axes.labelcolor": "#52514e", "axes.titlecolor": "#141413",
    "axes.titlesize": 13, "axes.spines.top": False, "axes.spines.right": False,
    "axes.grid": True, "grid.color": "#e6e5df", "grid.linewidth": 0.8,
    "axes.axisbelow": True,
    "xtick.color": "#898781", "ytick.color": "#898781",
    "xtick.labelsize": 10.5, "ytick.labelsize": 10.5,
    "legend.frameon": False, "legend.fontsize": 10.5,
    "figure.dpi": 100,
})
```

Eleven stocks, ten years, ranked by how much they move:

```{python}
#| echo: false
print((RET.std() * np.sqrt(252) * 100).sort_values(ascending=False)
      .round(1).to_string())
```

::: {.muted .small}
Annualised volatility, in percent. Three lines of pandas.
:::


# A question that ranking cannot answer

```{python}
#| echo: false
#| fig-align: center
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

s = WIDE["AAPL"].loc["2024-07-01":]
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(11.0, 2.7))
ax.plot(s.index, s.values, color=BLUE, linewidth=1.6)
ax.axvspan(s.index[-1], s.index[-1] + pd.Timedelta(days=45),
           color="#f2e6c9", zorder=0)
ax.text(s.index[-1] + pd.Timedelta(days=22), s.mean(), "?",
        ha="center", va="center", fontsize=30, color="#a8790b", fontweight="700")
ax.set_xlim(s.index[0], s.index[-1] + pd.Timedelta(days=45))
ax.set_title("Apple, closing price", loc="left")
ax.set_ylabel("price (USD)")
fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```

**Will tomorrow be an up day?** Nothing you have written so far even attempts
this. Every tool from Sessions 1 to 3 describes data that already happened.


# Describing, and predicting

<div class="compare">
<div class="compare-col">
<h3>Describing</h3>
<p>What did happen?</p>
<ul>
<li>Apple's volatility was 28.4% a year</li>
<li>Nvidia moved most, Coca-Cola least</li>
<li>The worst day was 16 March 2020</li>
</ul>
<p>You can check every one of these against the data.</p>
</div>
<div class="compare-col cmp-alt">
<h3>Predicting</h3>
<p>What will happen?</p>
<ul>
<li>Tomorrow's return will be about +0.3%</li>
<li>This client will default</li>
<li>This filing is unusual</li>
</ul>
<p>You cannot check any of them yet. That is the whole problem.</p>
</div>
</div>


# Today

A prediction needs four things: **a table of the right shape**, **a rule**, **a
way to score the rule**, and **data the rule has never seen**. Today is those
four things.

By the end of the session you can:

- put a problem into the **statistical learning framework**
- tell **supervised** from **unsupervised**, **regression** from
  **classification**
- handle **missing values, extreme values and columns on different scales**
- score a prediction with **MAE, MSE, RMSE, R²** or a **confusion matrix**
- explain **overfitting** and the **bias-variance trade-off** to somebody else


# This session is part maths

Sessions 1 to 3 were about writing code. This one is mostly about ideas, and
ideas here are written in symbols.

::: {.keypoint}
Nothing today is new maths. It is the vectors, matrices and sums you already
use in econometrics, written the way machine learning writes them, plus the
occasional expectation.
:::

::: {.muted .small}
We still write code, and there are live cells throughout. But **we do not fit a
model today.** That starts in the next block, once the ideas are in place.
:::


# 1 · The statistical learning framework {.divider}

::: {.divider-kicker}
Part 1
:::

::: {.divider-note}
Observations, features, targets, and the error nobody can remove.
:::


# It all starts with one table

::: {.diagram}
<svg width="1000" height="330" viewBox="0 0 1000 330" role="img" aria-label="A table whose rows are observations, whose first columns are features X, and whose last column is the target y">
  <text x="410" y="42" text-anchor="middle" font-size="17" font-family="Segoe UI, sans-serif" fill="#0d366b" font-weight="600">features</text>
  <text x="755" y="42" text-anchor="middle" font-size="17" font-family="Segoe UI, sans-serif" fill="#a8790b" font-weight="600">target</text>
  <g stroke="#86b6ef" stroke-width="1.4" fill="none">
    <path d="M 170 58 L 170 66 L 650 66 L 650 58"/>
  </g>
  <g stroke="#d99a1c" stroke-width="1.4" fill="none">
    <path d="M 680 58 L 680 66 L 830 66 L 830 58"/>
  </g>
  <rect x="170" y="82" width="480" height="34" fill="#eef4fc"/>
  <rect x="680" y="82" width="150" height="34" fill="#fbf3de"/>
  <rect x="680" y="116" width="150" height="180" fill="#fdfaf2"/>
  <g font-family="Cascadia Mono, monospace" font-size="14" fill="#0d366b">
    <text x="230" y="104" text-anchor="middle">ret_1d</text>
    <text x="350" y="104" text-anchor="middle">ret_5d</text>
    <text x="470" y="104" text-anchor="middle">vol_20d</text>
    <text x="590" y="104" text-anchor="middle">volume</text>
  </g>
  <text x="755" y="104" text-anchor="middle" font-family="Cascadia Mono, monospace" font-size="14" fill="#a8790b">ret_next</text>
  <text x="105" y="104" text-anchor="middle" font-family="Cascadia Mono, monospace" font-size="14" fill="#52514e">date</text>
  <g font-family="Cascadia Mono, monospace" font-size="13.5" fill="#141413">
    <text x="105" y="139" text-anchor="middle" fill="#52514e">2024-11-04</text>
    <text x="230" y="139" text-anchor="middle">-0.0018</text>
    <text x="350" y="139" text-anchor="middle">0.0121</text>
    <text x="470" y="139" text-anchor="middle">0.0143</text>
    <text x="590" y="139" text-anchor="middle">41.2M</text>
    <text x="755" y="139" text-anchor="middle" fill="#8a6508">0.0091</text>
    <text x="105" y="175" text-anchor="middle" fill="#52514e">2024-11-05</text>
    <text x="230" y="175" text-anchor="middle">0.0091</text>
    <text x="350" y="175" text-anchor="middle">0.0087</text>
    <text x="470" y="175" text-anchor="middle">0.0140</text>
    <text x="590" y="175" text-anchor="middle">28.1M</text>
    <text x="755" y="175" text-anchor="middle" fill="#8a6508">0.0042</text>
    <text x="105" y="211" text-anchor="middle" fill="#52514e">2024-11-06</text>
    <text x="230" y="211" text-anchor="middle">0.0042</text>
    <text x="350" y="211" text-anchor="middle">0.0102</text>
    <text x="470" y="211" text-anchor="middle">0.0138</text>
    <text x="590" y="211" text-anchor="middle">54.6M</text>
    <text x="755" y="211" text-anchor="middle" fill="#8a6508">-0.0033</text>
    <text x="105" y="247" text-anchor="middle" fill="#52514e">2024-11-07</text>
    <text x="230" y="247" text-anchor="middle">-0.0033</text>
    <text x="350" y="247" text-anchor="middle">0.0064</text>
    <text x="470" y="247" text-anchor="middle">0.0135</text>
    <text x="590" y="247" text-anchor="middle">42.1M</text>
    <text x="755" y="247" text-anchor="middle" fill="#8a6508">-0.0121</text>
    <text x="105" y="283" text-anchor="middle" fill="#52514e">2024-11-08</text>
    <text x="230" y="283" text-anchor="middle">-0.0121</text>
    <text x="350" y="283" text-anchor="middle">-0.0009</text>
    <text x="470" y="283" text-anchor="middle">0.0137</text>
    <text x="590" y="283" text-anchor="middle">38.3M</text>
    <text x="755" y="283" text-anchor="middle" fill="#8a6508">0.0107</text>
  </g>
  <g stroke="#e6e5df" stroke-width="1">
    <line x1="40" y1="116" x2="650" y2="116"/>
    <line x1="40" y1="152" x2="650" y2="152"/>
    <line x1="40" y1="188" x2="650" y2="188"/>
    <line x1="40" y1="224" x2="650" y2="224"/>
    <line x1="40" y1="260" x2="650" y2="260"/>
    <line x1="40" y1="296" x2="650" y2="296"/>
  </g>
  <g stroke="#e0d8c2" stroke-width="1">
    <line x1="680" y1="116" x2="830" y2="116"/>
    <line x1="680" y1="152" x2="830" y2="152"/>
    <line x1="680" y1="188" x2="830" y2="188"/>
    <line x1="680" y1="224" x2="830" y2="224"/>
    <line x1="680" y1="260" x2="830" y2="260"/>
    <line x1="680" y1="296" x2="830" y2="296"/>
  </g>
  <g stroke="#c3c2b7" stroke-width="1.2" fill="none">
    <path d="M 880 116 L 895 116 L 895 296 L 880 296"/>
  </g>
  <text x="910" y="200" font-size="14" font-family="Segoe UI, sans-serif" fill="#52514e">n rows</text>
  <text x="910" y="220" font-size="14" font-family="Segoe UI, sans-serif" fill="#52514e">= n obs.</text>
</svg>
:::

::: {.diagram-note}
Each row is one observation. Each feature column is something you know at that
moment. The target column is what you want to predict.
:::


# The same objects, three vocabularies

You already know most of these words. Machine learning just uses different ones.

| econometrics | machine learning | in this table |
|---|---|---|
| observation | sample, instance, example | one row, one date |
| independent variable, regressor | feature, predictor, input | `ret_1d`, `vol_20d` |
| dependent variable | target, label, response, output | `ret_next` |
| coefficient | weight, parameter | one number per feature |
| residual | error | what the rule got wrong |
| model | learner, estimator | the rule itself |

: {.vocab}


# Building that table, from prices

```{pyodide}
prices = pd.read_csv("data/prices.csv", parse_dates=["date"])
wide = prices.pivot(index="date", columns="ticker", values="close")
rets = wide.pct_change()

aapl = pd.DataFrame({
    "ret_1d": rets["AAPL"],
    "ret_5d": rets["AAPL"].rolling(5).mean(),
    "vol_20d": rets["AAPL"].rolling(20).std(),
})
aapl["ret_next"] = rets["AAPL"].shift(-1)
aapl = aapl.dropna()
aapl.tail(4)
```

::: {.muted .small}
`shift(-1)` pulls tomorrow's return back onto today's row. Every feature on a
row is known **before** the target happens. That is the rule that makes the
table honest.
:::


# n and p

The table has a standard notation, and it is the notation from Session 3.

::: {.formula}
$$
\mathbf{X} \in \mathbb{R}^{\,n \times p}, \qquad
\mathbf{y} \in \mathbb{R}^{\,n}
$$
:::

- $n$ is the number of **observations**, the rows
- $p$ is the number of **features**, the columns of $\mathbf{X}$
- $x_{ij}$ is the value of feature $j$ for observation $i$
- $y_i$ is the target for observation $i$

::: {.muted .small}
`aapl.shape` on the previous slide told you $n$ and $p+1$ directly. In NumPy
$\mathbf{X}$ is a 2-dimensional array and $\mathbf{y}$ is a 1-dimensional one.
:::


# The assumption underneath everything

::: {.formula}
$$
Y = \underbrace{f(X)}_{\text{signal}} \; + \; \underbrace{\varepsilon}_{\text{noise}}
$$
:::

There is some true relationship $f$ between the features and the target, and
whatever it does not account for is $\varepsilon$.

::: {.muted .small}
$f$ is fixed and unknown. $\varepsilon$ has mean zero and is independent of $X$.
Everything in the rest of this course is an attempt to get at $f$.
:::


# The part that is really there

```{python}
#| echo: false
#| fig-align: center
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(10.4, 3.4))
ax.plot(XGRID, true_f(XGRID), color=INK, linewidth=2.2, label="the true $f$")
ax.scatter(XT, YT, s=42, color=AMBER, zorder=3, label="what you observe")
ax.set_xlabel("feature $x$")
ax.set_ylabel("target $y$")
ax.set_title("An invented example, so that the truth can be drawn", loc="left")
ax.legend(loc="upper right")
fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```

::: {.muted .small}
Invented data. With real data nobody can draw the black line, which is exactly
why this picture is worth looking at now.
:::


# And the part that is not

```{python}
#| echo: false
#| fig-align: center
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(10.4, 3.4))
ax.plot(XGRID, true_f(XGRID), color=INK, linewidth=2.2, zorder=2)
ax.vlines(XT, true_f(XT), YT, color=BRICK, linewidth=1.4, zorder=1)
ax.scatter(XT, YT, s=42, color=AMBER, zorder=3)
ax.set_xlabel("feature $x$")
ax.set_ylabel("target $y$")
ax.set_title(r"Every red segment is one $\varepsilon_i$", loc="left")
fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```

$\varepsilon$ is measurement error, features you did not collect, and genuine
randomness, all at once. **In finance it is most of what you see.**


# What you actually get

```{python}
#| echo: false
#| fig-align: center
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(10.4, 3.4))
ax.scatter(XT, YT, s=42, color=AMBER, zorder=3)
ax.set_xlabel("feature $x$")
ax.set_ylabel("target $y$")
ax.set_title("The same data, without the answer key", loc="left")
ax.set_ylim(-1.9, 1.9)
fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```

Thirty dots. No curve, no formula, and no way to check your answer against the
truth. This is the situation you are always in.


# So you estimate it

```{python}
#| echo: false
#| fig-align: center
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from numpy.polynomial import Polynomial

fit3 = Polynomial.fit(XT, YT, 3)

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(10.4, 3.4))
ax.plot(XGRID, true_f(XGRID), color=GREY, linewidth=1.8, linestyle="--",
        label="the true $f$ (never visible)")
ax.plot(XGRID, fit3(XGRID), color=BLUE, linewidth=2.4, label=r"your $\hat{f}$")
ax.scatter(XT, YT, s=42, color=AMBER, zorder=3)
ax.set_xlabel("feature $x$")
ax.set_ylabel("target $y$")
ax.set_ylim(-1.9, 1.9)
ax.legend(loc="upper right")
fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```

$\hat{f}$ is your estimate, built from the data you have. A **model** is a
recipe for producing $\hat{f}$, and a **prediction** is $\hat{y} = \hat{f}(x)$.


# Two reasons to want it

<div class="compare">
<div class="compare-col">
<h3>Prediction</h3>
<p>You want the prediction to be close to the truth, and you do not care how.</p>
<ul>
<li>what will this loan lose?</li>
<li>will this trade be profitable?</li>
<li>is this transaction fraudulent?</li>
</ul>
<p>The rule is allowed to be a black box.</p>
</div>
<div class="compare-col cmp-alt">
<h3>Inference</h3>
<p>You want to know <strong>which features matter, and how</strong>.</p>
<ul>
<li>does size explain returns after controlling for value?</li>
<li>how much does one rating notch cost in spread?</li>
<li>which factor drives this portfolio?</li>
</ul>
<p>The rule has to be readable.</p>
</div>
</div>

::: {.muted .small}
Econometrics leans hard on the right-hand column. Machine learning leans hard
on the left. Same framework, different priority.
:::


# How wrong you can be, split in two

::: {.formula}
$$
\mathbb{E}\left[(Y - \hat{Y})^2\right] \;=\;
\underbrace{\left[f(X) - \hat{f}(X)\right]^2}_{\textbf{reducible}} \;+\;
\underbrace{\operatorname{Var}(\varepsilon)}_{\textbf{irreducible}}
$$
:::

<div class="compare">
<div class="compare-col">
<h3>Reducible</h3>
<p>The gap between your estimate and the truth. Better features, more data or a better model shrink it.</p>
</div>
<div class="compare-col cmp-alt">
<h3>Irreducible</h3>
<p>The noise itself. No model, no data and no amount of compute removes it. It is a property of the problem.</p>
</div>
</div>


# The floor, drawn

```{python}
#| echo: false
#| fig-align: center
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from numpy.polynomial import Polynomial

fit1 = Polynomial.fit(XT, YT, 1)
marks = np.linspace(0.06, 0.94, 12)

fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(11.0, 3.2), sharey=True)

ax = axes[0]
ax.fill_between(XGRID, fit1(XGRID), true_f(XGRID), color=BLUE, alpha=0.16,
                linewidth=0)
ax.plot(XGRID, true_f(XGRID), color=INK, linewidth=2.0, label="the true $f$")
ax.plot(XGRID, fit1(XGRID), color=BLUE, linewidth=2.2, label=r"your $\hat{f}$")
ax.text(0.30, 1.45, "the gap you can close", ha="center", fontsize=11.5,
        color=BLUE, fontweight="600")
ax.set_title("Reducible: your curve is not the true curve", loc="left",
             fontsize=12)
ax.set_ylabel("target $y$")
ax.legend(loc="lower left", fontsize=10)

ax = axes[1]
ax.vlines(XT, true_f(XT), YT, color=BRICK, linewidth=1.9)
ax.plot(XGRID, true_f(XGRID), color=INK, linewidth=2.0)
ax.scatter(XT, YT, s=26, color=AMBER, zorder=3)
ax.set_title("Irreducible: even the true curve misses every point", loc="left",
             fontsize=12)

for ax in axes:
    ax.set_xlabel("feature $x$")
    ax.set_ylim(-2.1, 2.1)
fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```

You can move the blue curve until it lies on top of the black one. **The red
segments would still be there**, because they are the $\varepsilon$ from
earlier: the part of $y$ that no feature in your table accounts for.


# In finance the floor is high

<div class="statband">
<div class="stat"><span class="stat-big">0.56</span><div class="stat-label">R&sup2; of Apple's return on the market's, <strong>same day</strong></div></div>
<div class="stat stat-warn"><span class="stat-big">0.01</span><div class="stat-label">R&sup2; of Apple's return on <strong>yesterday's</strong> return, in sample</div></div>
<div class="stat stat-warn"><span class="stat-big">-0.02</span><div class="stat-label">the same rule, on data it had not seen</div></div>
</div>

Explaining today with today is easy. Explaining tomorrow with today is close to
impossible, because $\operatorname{Var}(\varepsilon)$ is enormous.

::: {.muted .small}
That last number is not a typo. We come back to it in Part 4.
:::


# You have already estimated an $f$

Session 3 ended on this line:

```python
beta = np.linalg.inv(X.T @ X) @ X.T @ y
```

::: {.formula}
$$
\hat{f}(x) = \hat{\beta}_0 + \hat{\beta}_1 x,
\qquad
\hat{\boldsymbol\beta} = (\mathbf{X}^{\top}\mathbf{X})^{-1}\mathbf{X}^{\top}\mathbf{y}
$$
:::

Linear regression **is** a machine learning model: a rule with parameters,
chosen to fit a sample. It is the simplest one, and it is still the one to beat.


# Assume a shape, or do not

<div class="compare">
<div class="compare-col">
<h3>Parametric</h3>
<p>Pick a form first, then find its numbers.</p>
<p>Linear regression fixes one coefficient per feature, plus an intercept, whatever the sample size.</p>
<p><strong>Cheap, stable, easy to read. Wrong if the true shape is not that shape.</strong></p>
</div>
<div class="compare-col cmp-alt">
<h3>Non-parametric</h3>
<p>Let the data choose the shape.</p>
<p>Nearest neighbours, trees, splines. The rule grows with the sample.</p>
<p><strong>Flexible. Needs far more data, and is harder to explain.</strong></p>
</div>
</div>


# The same data, both ways

```{python}
#| echo: false
#| fig-align: center
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from numpy.polynomial import Polynomial

def knn_curve(xs, x, y, k):
    out = []
    for xi in xs:
        idx = np.argsort(np.abs(x - xi))[:k]
        out.append(y[idx].mean())
    return np.array(out)

fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(11.0, 3.1), sharey=True)
for ax, ttl in zip(axes, ["Parametric: a straight line",
                          "Non-parametric: 5 nearest neighbours"]):
    ax.scatter(XT, YT, s=30, color=GREY_LIGHT, zorder=2)
    ax.set_xlabel("feature $x$")
    ax.set_title(ttl, loc="left")
axes[0].plot(XGRID, Polynomial.fit(XT, YT, 1)(XGRID), color=BLUE, linewidth=2.4)
axes[1].plot(XGRID, knn_curve(XGRID, XT, YT, 5), color=AMBER, linewidth=2.4)
axes[0].set_ylabel("target $y$")
fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```

Two shapes for the two coefficients you get on the left. On the right there are
no coefficients at all: the rule is "average the five closest rows".


# The rows you learn from, and the rows you check on

A model has already seen the answers in the rows it was built from, and a
flexible enough rule can simply memorise them. So a score computed on those
rows is not evidence of anything.

::: {.diagram}
<svg width="1000" height="260" viewBox="0 0 1000 260" role="img" aria-label="The rows of the table split into a training block used to fit the model and a test block held back until the end">
  <rect x="150" y="40" width="420" height="118" rx="6" fill="#eef4fc" stroke="#1c5cab" stroke-width="1.6"/>
  <rect x="150" y="166" width="420" height="52" rx="6" fill="#fbf3de" stroke="#b8860b" stroke-width="1.6"/>
  <g stroke="#c7d8ee" stroke-width="1">
    <line x1="150" y1="70" x2="570" y2="70"/>
    <line x1="150" y1="100" x2="570" y2="100"/>
    <line x1="150" y1="130" x2="570" y2="130"/>
  </g>
  <line x1="150" y1="192" x2="570" y2="192" stroke="#e6d3a8" stroke-width="1"/>
  <text x="360" y="106" text-anchor="middle" font-size="19" font-family="Segoe UI, sans-serif" fill="#0d366b" font-weight="600">training set</text>
  <text x="360" y="198" text-anchor="middle" font-size="19" font-family="Segoe UI, sans-serif" fill="#8a6508" font-weight="600">test set</text>
  <text x="130" y="105" text-anchor="end" font-size="14" font-family="Segoe UI, sans-serif" fill="#898781">70 to 80%</text>
  <text x="130" y="198" text-anchor="end" font-size="14" font-family="Segoe UI, sans-serif" fill="#898781">the rest</text>
  <g stroke="#1c5cab" stroke-width="1.6" fill="none" marker-end="url(#arrowb)">
    <path d="M 590 99 L 700 99"/>
  </g>
  <g stroke="#b8860b" stroke-width="1.6" fill="none" stroke-dasharray="5 4">
    <path d="M 590 192 L 700 192"/>
  </g>
  <defs>
    <marker id="arrowb" markerWidth="9" markerHeight="9" refX="7" refY="3" orient="auto">
      <path d="M0,0 L0,6 L8,3 z" fill="#1c5cab"/>
    </marker>
  </defs>
  <polygon points="700,188 700,196 710,192" fill="#b8860b"/>
  <text x="720" y="93" font-size="15" font-family="Segoe UI, sans-serif" fill="#0d366b">fit the model,</text>
  <text x="720" y="113" font-size="15" font-family="Segoe UI, sans-serif" fill="#0d366b">choose everything</text>
  <text x="720" y="186" font-size="15" font-family="Segoe UI, sans-serif" fill="#8a6508">touched once,</text>
  <text x="720" y="206" font-size="15" font-family="Segoe UI, sans-serif" fill="#8a6508">at the very end</text>
</svg>
:::

::: {.muted .small}
**Why** you split is today's business, and it runs through the rest of the
session. **How** to split well, and how to reuse the training rows without ever
touching the test rows, is model selection and cross-validation in the next
block.
:::


# Your turn [1]{.chip .chip-turn} {.your-turn}

`rets["MSFT"]` is a Series of daily returns. Build a two-column table whose
target is **the return five days ahead**.

```{pyodide}
#| autorun: false
msft = pd.DataFrame({"ret_1d": rets["MSFT"]})

msft["ret_5d_ahead"] = ...
msft = msft.dropna()
msft.tail(3)
```


# Your turn 1: answer

```python
msft = pd.DataFrame({"ret_1d": rets["MSFT"]})

msft["ret_5d_ahead"] = rets["MSFT"].shift(-5)
msft = msft.dropna()
msft.tail(3)
```

**A negative shift moves the future backwards onto today's row.** Shifting the
wrong way gives you yesterday's return as a target, which you could predict
perfectly and which would be worth nothing.


# 2 · What kind of problem is it? {.divider}

::: {.divider-kicker}
Part 2
:::

::: {.divider-note}
Supervised, unsupervised, and the one we are not covering.
:::


# One question decides almost everything

::: {.diagram}
<svg width="1000" height="320" viewBox="0 0 1000 320" role="img" aria-label="Decision tree: if the table has a target column the problem is supervised, and splits into regression for a numeric target and classification for a label; if not, it is unsupervised, and splits into clustering and dimension reduction">
  <rect x="330" y="8" width="340" height="46" rx="7" fill="#eef4fc" stroke="#1c5cab" stroke-width="1.6"/>
  <text x="500" y="37" text-anchor="middle" font-size="17" font-family="Segoe UI, sans-serif" fill="#0d366b" font-weight="600">Is there a target column?</text>
  <g stroke="#c3c2b7" stroke-width="1.4" fill="none">
    <path d="M 500 54 L 500 72 L 230 72 L 230 96"/>
    <path d="M 500 54 L 500 72 L 780 72 L 780 96"/>
    <path d="M 230 142 L 230 160 L 120 160 L 120 186"/>
    <path d="M 230 142 L 230 160 L 340 160 L 340 186"/>
    <path d="M 780 142 L 780 160 L 670 160 L 670 186"/>
    <path d="M 780 142 L 780 160 L 890 160 L 890 186"/>
  </g>
  <text x="350" y="90" text-anchor="middle" font-size="14" font-family="Segoe UI, sans-serif" fill="#1b7a4b" font-weight="600">yes</text>
  <text x="660" y="90" text-anchor="middle" font-size="14" font-family="Segoe UI, sans-serif" fill="#b3402f" font-weight="600">no</text>
  <rect x="110" y="96" width="240" height="46" rx="7" fill="#ffffff" stroke="#1c5cab" stroke-width="1.6"/>
  <text x="230" y="125" text-anchor="middle" font-size="17" font-family="Segoe UI, sans-serif" fill="#0d366b" font-weight="600">Supervised</text>
  <rect x="660" y="96" width="240" height="46" rx="7" fill="#ffffff" stroke="#b8860b" stroke-width="1.6"/>
  <text x="780" y="125" text-anchor="middle" font-size="17" font-family="Segoe UI, sans-serif" fill="#8a6508" font-weight="600">Unsupervised</text>
  <rect x="20" y="186" width="200" height="42" rx="7" fill="#f6f9fd" stroke="#86b6ef" stroke-width="1.4"/>
  <text x="120" y="213" text-anchor="middle" font-size="15.5" font-family="Segoe UI, sans-serif" fill="#0d366b">Regression</text>
  <rect x="240" y="186" width="200" height="42" rx="7" fill="#f6f9fd" stroke="#86b6ef" stroke-width="1.4"/>
  <text x="340" y="213" text-anchor="middle" font-size="15.5" font-family="Segoe UI, sans-serif" fill="#0d366b">Classification</text>
  <rect x="570" y="186" width="200" height="42" rx="7" fill="#fdf8ed" stroke="#e0c27a" stroke-width="1.4"/>
  <text x="670" y="213" text-anchor="middle" font-size="15.5" font-family="Segoe UI, sans-serif" fill="#8a6508">Clustering</text>
  <rect x="790" y="186" width="200" height="42" rx="7" fill="#fdf8ed" stroke="#e0c27a" stroke-width="1.4"/>
  <text x="890" y="213" text-anchor="middle" font-size="15.5" font-family="Segoe UI, sans-serif" fill="#8a6508">Dimension reduction</text>
  <g font-size="13" font-family="Segoe UI, sans-serif" fill="#898781">
    <text x="120" y="256" text-anchor="middle">the target is</text>
    <text x="120" y="274" text-anchor="middle">a number</text>
    <text x="340" y="256" text-anchor="middle">the target is</text>
    <text x="340" y="274" text-anchor="middle">a label</text>
    <text x="670" y="256" text-anchor="middle">which rows</text>
    <text x="670" y="274" text-anchor="middle">resemble each other</text>
    <text x="890" y="256" text-anchor="middle">fewer columns,</text>
    <text x="890" y="274" text-anchor="middle">same information</text>
  </g>
</svg>
:::


# Supervised: you have the answers

For every row in the training data, somebody has already written down the
target.

::: {.why-grid}
::: {.why-point}
**Credit** <span>past loans, and whether each one defaulted</span>
:::
::: {.why-point}
**Trading** <span>past days, and what the return turned out to be</span>
:::
::: {.why-point}
**Fraud** <span>past transactions, and which were later confirmed as fraud</span>
:::
::: {.why-point}
**Ratings** <span>past issuers, and the rating each was given</span>
:::
:::

::: {.keypoint}
The target column is the expensive part. Somebody had to observe it, or label
it by hand, and there is usually far less of it than you would like.
:::


# Regression: the target is a number

```{python}
#| echo: false
#| fig-align: center
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

x = RET["SPY"].values
y = RET["AAPL"].values
b1, b0 = np.polyfit(x, y, 1)
grid = np.linspace(x.min(), x.max(), 50)

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(9.6, 3.2))
ax.scatter(x * 100, y * 100, s=7, color=BLUE, alpha=0.28, linewidths=0)
ax.plot(grid * 100, (b0 + b1 * grid) * 100, color=INK, linewidth=2.0)
ax.set_xlabel("market return that day (%)")
ax.set_ylabel("Apple return (%)")
ax.set_title("2,515 days. Predict a number: how much did Apple move?", loc="left")
fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```

The answer can be any value on the vertical axis. **This is the OLS picture you
already know**, seen as a prediction problem.


# Classification: the target is a label

```{python}
#| echo: false
#| fig-align: center
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

x = RET["SPY"].values
y = RET["AAPL"].values
up = y > 0

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(9.6, 3.2))
ax.scatter(x[up] * 100, np.full(up.sum(), 1), s=9, color=BLUE, alpha=0.30,
           linewidths=0, label="Apple up")
ax.scatter(x[~up] * 100, np.full((~up).sum(), 0), s=9, color=BRICK, alpha=0.30,
           linewidths=0, label="Apple down")
ax.set_yticks([0, 1])
ax.set_yticklabels(["down", "up"])
ax.set_ylim(-0.6, 1.6)
ax.set_xlabel("market return that day (%)")
ax.set_title("The same 2,515 days. Predict a label: up or down?", loc="left")
ax.legend(loc="center left")
fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```

Now there are only two possible answers, and being "nearly right" is not a
thing.


# The same data, two different problems

::: {.keypoint}
Nothing changed in the data. **You chose** what the target would be, and that
choice changes the model you can use, the metric you score it with, and what a
mistake costs you.
:::

::: {.why-grid}
::: {.why-point}
**As regression** <span>predict the return, then act on its size</span>
:::
::: {.why-point}
**As classification** <span>predict the direction, then act on the direction</span>
:::
:::

::: {.muted .small}
Turning a number into a label always throws information away. A +0.02% day and
a +6% day become the same "up". Sometimes that is the right trade, because
direction is easier to get right than size.
:::


# Making the label yourself

```{pyodide}
labels = (rets["AAPL"] > 0).astype(int)

print(labels.tail(6).to_string())
print()
print("share of up days:", round(labels.mean(), 4))
```

::: {.muted .small}
A comparison gives `True` and `False`, and `.astype(int)` turns them into 1 and
0. **53% of days are up**, which will matter a great deal when we start scoring
classifiers.
:::


# Unsupervised: nobody wrote the answers down

There is no target column. You have $\mathbf{X}$ and nothing else, and the
question becomes "what is the structure in here?"

::: {.why-grid}
::: {.why-point}
**Clustering** <span>which of these stocks behave alike, without being told the sectors</span>
:::
::: {.why-point}
**Dimension reduction** <span>500 correlated series, and three factors that carry most of it</span>
:::
::: {.why-point}
**Anomaly detection** <span>which trades do not look like any of the others</span>
:::
::: {.why-point}
**Topic models** <span>what are these ten thousand filings about</span>
:::
:::


# Eleven stocks, no target

```{python}
#| echo: false
#| fig-align: center
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

vol = RET.std() * np.sqrt(252) * 100
mean = RET.mean() * 252 * 100

# Hand-placed labels: four of the eleven sit almost on top of each other.
PLACE = {
    "SPY": (-8, 9, "right"), "KO": (-9, -5, "right"), "JNJ": (0, -17, "center"),
    "PG": (9, -3, "left"), "WMT": (0, 11, "center"), "MSFT": (-9, 4, "right"),
    "AAPL": (9, 2, "left"), "JPM": (9, -3, "left"), "XOM": (9, -3, "left"),
    "DIS": (-9, -4, "right"), "NVDA": (0, 12, "center"),
}

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(9.8, 3.4))
ax.scatter(vol, mean, s=95, color=BLUE, zorder=3)
for t in RET.columns:
    dx, dy, ha = PLACE[t]
    ax.annotate(t, (vol[t], mean[t]), xytext=(dx, dy), textcoords="offset points",
                ha=ha, fontsize=11, color="#52514e")
ax.set_xlabel("annualised volatility (%)")
ax.set_ylabel("annualised mean return (%)")
ax.set_xlim(13, 54)
ax.set_ylim(0, 84)
ax.set_title("Two features, no answer key", loc="left")
fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```

Nobody labelled these. You can still see that the defensive names sit together
in the bottom left, and Nvidia sits alone. **That is clustering, done by eye.**


# The catch with unsupervised work

<div class="compare">
<div class="compare-col">
<h3>Supervised</h3>
<p>There is a right answer, so there is a score.</p>
<p>You can say "this model is better than that one" and defend it with a number.</p>
</div>
<div class="compare-col cmp-alt">
<h3>Unsupervised</h3>
<p>There is no right answer, so there is no score.</p>
<p>Three clusters or five? Both are defensible. You are back to judgement.</p>
</div>
</div>

::: {.muted .small}
This is why supervised learning dominates in practice, and why most of this
course is about it.
:::


# Reinforcement learning

The third paradigm. No fixed table at all: an **agent** takes actions in an
environment, gets rewards, and learns a policy that earns more of them.

::: {.why-grid}
::: {.why-point}
**In principle** <span>optimal execution, hedging, market making, portfolio rebalancing</span>
:::
::: {.why-point}
**In practice** <span>it needs an environment to practise in, and markets do not offer one</span>
:::
:::

::: {.keypoint}
**We do not cover reinforcement learning in this course.** You should know the
word, and know that it is a different setting: the data depends on what the
agent decided to do.
:::


# Your turn [2]{.chip .chip-turn} {.your-turn}

Which is each of these?

1. Predict a bond's yield spread next month
2. Group 3,000 funds by how they behave, with no style labels
3. Decide whether a filing will be followed by a restatement
4. Compress 60 macro series into a handful of factors
5. Predict how many basis points a stock moves after an earnings surprise


# Your turn 2: answer

1. Predict a spread ➜ **regression**, the target is a number
2. Group funds ➜ **unsupervised**, clustering, no target exists
3. Restatement or not ➜ **classification**, the target is a label
4. Compress 60 series ➜ **unsupervised**, dimension reduction
5. Basis points after a surprise ➜ **regression**, the target is a number

::: {.muted .small}
Number 3 could be made a regression by predicting the probability instead. The
line is not always sharp, and the decision is yours to defend.
:::


# 3 · Real data is never that shape {.divider}

::: {.divider-kicker}
Part 3
:::

::: {.divider-note}
Missing values, extreme values, and columns measured in different worlds.
:::


# Missing values, and where they come from

::: {.why-grid}
::: {.why-point}
**Calendars disagree** <span>US and European exchanges keep different holidays, so a merged table has gaps</span>
:::
::: {.why-point}
**The company was not there** <span>an IPO in 2019 has no 2015 rows, and a delisted firm has no 2024 rows</span>
:::
::: {.why-point}
**Trading stopped** <span>halts, suspensions, and days with no quote</span>
:::
::: {.why-point}
**Reporting is optional** <span>a fund reports until the month it would rather not</span>
:::
:::

::: {.muted .small}
The last one is the dangerous one, and we come back to it.
:::


# Finding them

```{pyodide}
calendar = pd.date_range(wide.index.min(), wide.index.max(), freq="D")
daily = wide.reindex(calendar)

print("rows before:", len(wide))
print("rows after :", len(daily))
print()
print(daily.isna().sum().head(4).to_string())
```

::: {.muted .small}
`reindex` forces the table onto a row per calendar day, which is what happens
the moment you merge with anything daily. **1,136 of 3,652 days are now empty**,
and `.isna().sum()` counts them column by column.
:::


# A picture of it

```{python}
#| echo: false
#| fig-align: center
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

# One cell per day of 2024: weekday down, week across. The cause of the
# missingness is then impossible to miss.
cal = pd.date_range("2024-01-01", "2024-12-31", freq="D")
missing = ~cal.isin(WIDE.index)
weeks = ((cal - cal[0]).days + cal[0].weekday()) // 7
grid_ = np.full((7, weeks.max() + 1), np.nan)
for d, m, w in zip(cal, missing, weeks):
    grid_[d.weekday(), w] = 1.0 if m else 0.0

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(11.0, 2.5))
ax.imshow(grid_, aspect="auto", interpolation="nearest",
          cmap=mpl.colors.ListedColormap(["#dde7f4", BRICK]))
ax.set_yticks(range(7))
ax.set_yticklabels(["Mon", "Tue", "Wed", "Thu", "Fri", "Sat", "Sun"], fontsize=10)
month_starts = [(w, d.strftime("%b")) for d, w in zip(cal, weeks) if d.day == 1]
ax.set_xticks([w for w, _ in month_starts])
ax.set_xticklabels([m for _, m in month_starts], fontsize=10)
ax.grid(False)
ax.set_title("Every day of 2024. Red means no row in the price table", loc="left")
fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```

Two solid rows of weekends, and **ten single red squares**: New Year, Martin
Luther King Day, Presidents' Day, Good Friday, and the rest of the market
holidays. A picture of missingness usually tells you the cause immediately,
which is what you need before you decide what to do about it.


# Three ways out

::: {.why-grid}
::: {.why-point}
**Drop the rows** <span>`df.dropna()`. Safe when the gaps are few and unrelated to what you are studying.</span>
:::
::: {.why-point}
**Drop the column** <span>if a feature is 60% empty it is usually not worth rescuing.</span>
:::
::: {.why-point}
**Fill them in** <span>`fillna`, `ffill`, `interpolate`. You are inventing data, so be deliberate about it.</span>
:::
::: {.why-point}
**Keep the fact** <span>add a column that records "this was missing". Sometimes that is the signal.</span>
:::
:::


# What each choice looks like

```{python}
#| echo: false
#| fig-align: center
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

seg = WIDE["AAPL"].loc["2024-04-01":"2024-06-28"]
_r = np.random.default_rng(3)
gaps = np.sort(_r.choice(np.arange(5, len(seg) - 5), 14, replace=False))
obs = seg.copy()
obs.iloc[gaps] = np.nan
truth = seg.iloc[gaps]

panels = [
    # plotted with the NaNs left in, so the line genuinely breaks where the
    # rows were dropped
    ("Drop the rows", obs, None),
    ("Fill with the mean", obs.fillna(obs.mean()), obs.fillna(obs.mean()).iloc[gaps]),
    ("Forward fill", obs.ffill(), obs.ffill().iloc[gaps]),
    ("Interpolate", obs.interpolate(), obs.interpolate().iloc[gaps]),
]

fig, axes = plt.subplots(2, 2, figsize=(11.0, 4.0), sharex=True, sharey=True)
for ax, (name, series, guess) in zip(axes.ravel(), panels):
    ax.plot(seg.index, seg.values, color="#b9b7ab", linewidth=1.1,
            linestyle="--", zorder=1)
    ax.plot(series.index, series.values, color=BLUE, linewidth=1.5, zorder=2)
    ax.scatter(obs.dropna().index, obs.dropna().values, s=11, color=BLUE,
               zorder=3)
    if guess is not None:
        ax.scatter(guess.index, guess.values, s=26, color=BRICK, marker="x",
                   zorder=4)
    ax.set_title(name, loc="left", fontsize=12)
    ax.tick_params(labelsize=9)
for ax in axes[:, 0]:
    ax.set_ylabel("price (USD)")
fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```

::: {.muted .small}
Grey dashed is the truth, red crosses are the invented values. Fourteen days
removed from Apple's spring.
:::


# The accurate answer is the illegal one

<div class="statband">
<div class="stat stat-warn"><span class="stat-big">$10.86</span><div class="stat-label">mean error, fill with the column mean</div></div>
<div class="stat"><span class="stat-big">$2.76</span><div class="stat-label">mean error, forward fill</div></div>
<div class="stat"><span class="stat-big">$1.67</span><div class="stat-label">mean error, interpolate</div></div>
</div>

Interpolation wins, because it draws a line between the price **before** the gap
and the price **after** it.

::: {.keypoint}
On 3 May you would not have known 8 May's price. Interpolation quietly puts
tomorrow's information into today's row, and every result built on it is
optimistic. **Forward fill only ever looks backwards.**
:::


# Why it is missing decides what you may do

<div class="compare">
<div class="compare-col">
<h3>Missing for an unrelated reason</h3>
<p>Thanksgiving. A vendor outage. A field nobody filled in.</p>
<p>Dropping those rows costs you sample size and nothing else.</p>
</div>
<div class="compare-col cmp-alt">
<h3>Missing because of the value</h3>
<p>The fund stopped reporting after the month it lost 40%. The firm delisted because it failed.</p>
<p>Drop those rows and your sample is only the survivors.</p>
</div>
</div>

::: {.muted .small}
The second case has a name in finance: **survivorship bias**. It is the reason a
backtest on today's index members looks so good.
:::


# Extreme values

```{python}
#| echo: false
#| fig-align: center
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

a = RET["AAPL"]
big = a[a.abs() > 0.07]

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(11.0, 2.9))
ax.plot(a.index, a.values * 100, color=BLUE_LIGHT, linewidth=0.7)
ax.scatter(big.index, big.values * 100, s=40, color=BRICK, zorder=3,
           marker="x", linewidths=1.6)
ax.axhline(0, color=GREY, linewidth=0.9)
ax.set_ylabel("daily return (%)")
ax.set_title("Apple, every day 2015 to 2024. Red: moves beyond 7%", loc="left")
fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```

Most of them are in March 2020, and they are not mistakes. **The worst day was
-12.9%, the best was +12.0%, and they were eleven days apart.**


# An error, or the whole point?

<div class="compare">
<div class="compare-col">
<h3>Probably an error</h3>
<p>A price of 0, or of 1,000,000. A return of +4,000% because of an unadjusted stock split. A date in 1901.</p>
<p>Fix it or drop it, and write down what you did.</p>
</div>
<div class="compare-col cmp-alt">
<h3>Probably the point</h3>
<p>March 2020. The 2008 quarter. The day the takeover was announced.</p>
<p>Delete these and you have built a model of a market where nothing ever happens.</p>
</div>
</div>

::: {.keypoint}
In most fields the extremes are noise. **In finance they are usually the
phenomenon.** Risk lives in the tail, so removing the tail removes the subject.
:::


# Two rules of thumb, and their limits

```{python}
#| echo: false
#| fig-align: center
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

a = RET["AAPL"] * 100
q1, q3 = a.quantile([0.25, 0.75])
iqr = q3 - q1
lo, hi = q1 - 1.5 * iqr, q3 + 1.5 * iqr

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(10.8, 2.9))
bp = ax.boxplot(a.values, vert=False, widths=0.42, whis=1.5,
                showfliers=True, patch_artist=True)
bp["boxes"][0].set(facecolor="#eef4fc", edgecolor=BLUE, linewidth=1.4)
for part in ("whiskers", "caps"):
    for line in bp[part]:
        line.set(color=BLUE, linewidth=1.2)
bp["medians"][0].set(color=INK, linewidth=1.6)
for f in bp["fliers"]:
    f.set(marker="o", markersize=3, markerfacecolor=BRICK,
          markeredgecolor="none", alpha=0.45)

# Name the parts, since the whole rule is built out of them. The box is narrow
# next to the outliers, so the labels go outside it rather than on top.
ax.text(q1 - 0.35, 0.70, "$Q_1$", ha="right", va="center", fontsize=12,
        color=INK)
ax.text(q3 + 0.35, 0.70, "$Q_3$", ha="left", va="center", fontsize=12,
        color=INK)
ax.annotate("", xy=(q1, 0.70), xytext=(q3, 0.70),
            arrowprops=dict(arrowstyle="<->", color=AMBER, linewidth=1.6))
ax.text((q1 + q3) / 2, 0.56, f"IQR = {iqr:.2f}", ha="center", fontsize=11.5,
        color="#8a6508", fontweight="600")
for x, lab in [(lo, f"{lo:.2f}%"), (hi, f"{hi:.2f}%")]:
    ax.axvline(x, color=BRICK, linewidth=1.2, linestyle="--")
    ax.text(x, 1.40, lab, ha="center", fontsize=11, color=BRICK)
ax.set_yticks([])
ax.set_ylim(0.42, 1.52)
ax.set_xlabel("Apple daily return (%)")
ax.set_title("Everything outside the dashed lines is flagged as an outlier",
             loc="left")
fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```

$Q_1$ and $Q_3$ are the 25th and 75th percentiles, and the **interquartile
range** $\text{IQR} = Q_3 - Q_1$ is the width of the middle half of the data.

::: {.formula}
$$
\text{IQR rule: outside } [\,Q_1 - 1.5\,\text{IQR},\; Q_3 + 1.5\,\text{IQR}\,]
\qquad
\text{z-score rule: } \left|\frac{x - \bar{x}}{s}\right| > 3
$$
:::


# Both rules flag far too much

<div class="statband">
<div class="stat stat-warn"><span class="stat-big">138</span><div class="stat-label">days flagged by the IQR rule, 5.5% of the sample</div></div>
<div class="stat stat-warn"><span class="stat-big">36</span><div class="stat-label">days beyond three standard deviations</div></div>
<div class="stat"><span class="stat-big">7</span><div class="stat-label">days a normal distribution says you should get</div></div>
</div>

The rules assume a bell curve. Returns are not one, so the rules mark hundreds
of perfectly real days as errors.

::: {.muted .small}
There is also a circular problem: one enormous day inflates $s$, which then
makes that day look less extreme.
:::


# The tails are the whole story

```{python}
#| echo: false
#| fig-align: center
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

a = RET["AAPL"].values * 100
grid = np.linspace(-14, 14, 400)
normal = (1 / (a.std() * np.sqrt(2 * np.pi))) * np.exp(-0.5 * ((grid - a.mean()) / a.std()) ** 2)

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(10.6, 3.0))
ax.hist(a, bins=90, density=True, color=BLUE_LIGHT, edgecolor="none")
ax.plot(grid, normal, color=BRICK, linewidth=2.0,
        label="a normal with the same mean and standard deviation")
ax.set_yscale("log")
ax.set_xlabel("Apple daily return (%)")
ax.set_ylabel("density (log scale)")
ax.set_title("Real returns against the bell curve", loc="left")
ax.legend(loc="upper right")
fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```

The bars sit far above the red line at both ends. **Days that "cannot happen"
happen several times a decade**, and they are the ones that decide whether a
strategy survives.


# So what do you actually do

::: {.why-grid}
::: {.why-point}
**Check the source first** <span>most true errors are visible in one plot or one `.describe()`</span>
:::
::: {.why-point}
**Do not delete by default** <span>a rule that flags 5% of your days is not finding errors, it is finding the market</span>
:::
::: {.why-point}
**Score with a robust metric** <span>MAE cares far less about one bad day than MSE does. Part 4.</span>
:::
::: {.why-point}
**Or transform** <span>a log squeezes the long right tail of prices, volumes and market caps</span>
:::
:::

::: {.keypoint}
Whatever you choose, **write it down**. "We dropped returns beyond ±20%, three
observations" is a sentence that belongs in the report.
:::


# Levels, and changes

```{python}
#| echo: false
#| fig-align: center
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(11.0, 2.9))
axes[0].plot(WIDE.index, WIDE["AAPL"], color=BLUE, linewidth=1.2)
axes[0].set_title("The level: price", loc="left")
axes[0].set_ylabel("USD")
axes[1].plot(RET.index, RET["AAPL"] * 100, color=BLUE_LIGHT, linewidth=0.6)
axes[1].axhline(0, color=GREY, linewidth=0.9)
axes[1].set_title("The change: daily return", loc="left")
axes[1].set_ylabel("%")
fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```

The left panel wanders off and never comes back, so its mean means nothing. The
right panel hovers around a fixed level, so its mean and standard deviation
describe something real. **Model the changes, not the levels.**


# Why logs

::: {.formula}
$$
r_t^{\log} = \log\!\left(\frac{P_t}{P_{t-1}}\right) = \log P_t - \log P_{t-1}
$$
:::

::: {.why-grid}
::: {.why-point}
**They add up** <span>a year's log return is the sum of its days. Simple returns have to be compounded.</span>
:::
::: {.why-point}
**They are symmetric** <span>-50% then +100% gets you back to where you started, and in logs those cancel exactly</span>
:::
::: {.why-point}
**They tame right skew** <span>market cap, volume and price are all long-tailed, and a log makes them workable</span>
:::
::: {.why-point}
**They are nearly the same** <span>for small daily moves, `log(1 + r)` and `r` agree to three decimals</span>
:::
:::


# Columns from different worlds

```{python}
#| echo: false
#| fig-align: center
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

aapl_vol = PX[PX["ticker"] == "AAPL"].set_index("date")["volume"]
scales = {
    "daily return": RET["AAPL"].std(),
    "price (USD)": WIDE["AAPL"].std(),
    "volume (shares)": aapl_vol.std(),
}
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(10.4, 2.3))
ax.barh(list(scales), list(scales.values()), color=BLUE, height=0.55)
ax.set_xscale("log")
ax.set_xlabel("standard deviation of the column (log scale)")
ax.set_title("Three features of the same stock", loc="left")
for i, v in enumerate(scales.values()):
    ax.text(v * 1.5, i, f"{v:,.3g}", va="center", fontsize=11, color="#52514e")
fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```

The volume column varies about **four billion times** more than the return
column. Any method that measures distance, or penalises large coefficients, will
hear only the volume.


# Standardising

::: {.formula}
$$
z_i = \frac{x_i - \bar{x}}{s}
$$
:::

```{pyodide}
vol20 = rets["AAPL"].rolling(20).std().dropna()
z = (vol20 - vol20.mean()) / vol20.std()

print("original : mean %.5f  sd %.5f" % (vol20.mean(), vol20.std()))
print("z-scored : mean %.5f  sd %.5f" % (z.mean(), z.std()))
```

::: {.muted .small}
Every column now has mean 0 and standard deviation 1, so a coefficient of 0.4
means the same thing in every column. OLS does not need this. Nearest
neighbours, penalised regression and neural networks all do.
:::


# Columns that are not numbers

```{pyodide}
firms = pd.DataFrame({
    "ticker": ["AAPL", "KO", "JPM", "XOM"],
    "sector": ["Tech", "Staples", "Financials", "Energy"],
})

pd.get_dummies(firms, columns=["sector"], dtype=int)
```

::: {.muted .small}
One column of text becomes one 0/1 column per category. This is **one-hot
encoding**, and it is how a sector, a rating or a country gets into
$\mathbf{X}$.
:::


# The rule that governs all of it

::: {.keypoint}
Every number you use to clean the data, the mean you filled with, the standard
deviation you divided by, the quantiles you flagged outliers with, **must be
computed on the training rows alone.**
:::

Those are the rows from Part 1 that the model is allowed to learn from.
Compute the mean over the whole sample instead and it already contains the
period you were going to test on, so your test score flatters you and the model
disappoints in production.

::: {.muted .small}
It is the same look-ahead problem as interpolating across a gap, and it is the
single most common mistake in student projects and in published finance papers
alike.
:::


# Your turn [3]{.chip .chip-turn} {.your-turn}

Apply the IQR rule to Microsoft's returns, and count how many days it flags.

```{pyodide}
#| autorun: false
msft_ret = rets["MSFT"].dropna()
q1, q3 = msft_ret.quantile([0.25, 0.75])

iqr = ...
flagged = ...

print("days flagged:", flagged)
print("out of       :", len(msft_ret))
```


# Your turn 3: answer

```python
msft_ret = rets["MSFT"].dropna()
q1, q3 = msft_ret.quantile([0.25, 0.75])

iqr = q3 - q1
flagged = ((msft_ret < q1 - 1.5 * iqr) | (msft_ret > q3 + 1.5 * iqr)).sum()

print("days flagged:", flagged)
print("out of       :", len(msft_ret))
```

**134 days of 2,515, or 5.3%.** Almost exactly what the rule flagged for Apple,
and far too many to be errors. The fences sit at -3.2% and +3.5%, so the rule is
calling an ordinary bad Tuesday an outlier.


# 4 · Was the prediction any good? {.divider}

::: {.divider-kicker}
Part 4
:::

::: {.divider-note}
Turning a column of mistakes into one number you can defend.
:::


# A rule, and what it predicted

Session 3 fitted Apple's return against the market's and got two numbers. That
is a model:

::: {.formula}
$$
\hat{y}_t = 0.00043 + 1.2073 \, x_t
$$
:::

```{pyodide}
market = rets["SPY"].dropna()
actual = rets["AAPL"].dropna()

predicted = 0.00043 + 1.2073 * market
resid = actual - predicted

pd.DataFrame({"actual": actual, "predicted": predicted, "resid": resid}).tail(4)
```


# The residual

::: {.formula}
$$
e_i = y_i - \hat{y}_i
$$
:::

```{python}
#| echo: false
#| fig-align: center
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

sub = RET.loc["2024-10":"2024-12"]
xs = sub["SPY"].values * 100
ys = sub["AAPL"].values * 100
pred = (0.00043 + 1.2073 * sub["SPY"].values) * 100
grid = np.linspace(xs.min() - 0.2, xs.max() + 0.2, 40)

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(10.4, 3.0))
ax.plot(grid, 0.043 + 1.2073 * grid, color=INK, linewidth=1.8, zorder=2)
ax.vlines(xs, pred, ys, color=BRICK, linewidth=1.3, zorder=1)
ax.scatter(xs, ys, s=34, color=AMBER, zorder=3)
ax.set_xlabel("market return (%)")
ax.set_ylabel("Apple return (%)")
ax.set_title("The last quarter of 2024. Each red segment is one residual",
             loc="left")
fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```


# Why you cannot just add them up

```{pyodide}
print("sum of residuals  :", round(resid.sum(), 4))
print("mean residual     :", round(resid.mean(), 8))
print("mean size of one  :", round(resid.abs().mean(), 8))
```

::: {.muted .small}
The average residual is four millionths. The average residual **size** is more
than two thousand times bigger. Least squares picked the line that makes the
positives and the negatives cancel, so **the sum tells you nothing at all about
how wrong the model was.**
:::

You need to remove the signs first. There are two standard ways to do that.


# Mean absolute error

::: {.formula}
$$
\text{MAE} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n}\left|\,y_i - \hat{y}_i\,\right|
$$
:::

The average size of a mistake, in the units of the target.

::: {.muted .small}
For this model, 0.83%. On a typical day the rule is wrong by about eight tenths
of a percentage point, and that sentence is understandable by anybody.
:::


# Mean squared error, and its root

::: {.formula}
$$
\text{MSE} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n}\left(y_i - \hat{y}_i\right)^2
\qquad
\text{RMSE} = \sqrt{\text{MSE}}
$$
:::

Squaring removes the sign too, but it also **punishes a big miss much harder
than several small ones**.

::: {.muted .small}
MSE is in squared units, which nobody can interpret: 0.000142 squared returns.
Taking the root puts it back into percent, which is why RMSE is the one you
report.
:::


# All three, on the real residuals

```{pyodide}
mae = resid.abs().mean()
mse = (resid ** 2).mean()
rmse = np.sqrt(mse)

print("MAE  %.5f   (%.2f%%)" % (mae, mae * 100))
print("MSE  %.6f" % mse)
print("RMSE %.5f   (%.2f%%)" % (rmse, rmse * 100))
```

::: {.muted .small}
RMSE is always at least as large as MAE. The gap between them tells you how
uneven the errors are.
:::


# Why both exist

```{python}
#| echo: false
#| fig-align: center
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

e = np.linspace(-4, 4, 300)
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(9.6, 2.9))
ax.plot(e, np.abs(e), color=BLUE, linewidth=2.4, label="absolute error  $|e|$")
ax.plot(e, e ** 2, color=BRICK, linewidth=2.4, label="squared error  $e^2$")
ax.set_xlabel("error")
ax.set_ylabel("penalty")
ax.set_ylim(0, 9)
ax.legend(loc="upper center")
ax.set_title("What each metric charges you for being wrong", loc="left")
fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```

Being wrong by 4 costs **four times** as much as being wrong by 1 under MAE, and
**sixteen times** as much under MSE.


# One terrible forecast

```{python}
#| echo: false
#| fig-align: center
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

sub = RET.tail(60)
ya = sub["AAPL"].values * 100
yp = (0.00043 + 1.2073 * sub["SPY"].values) * 100
yp_bad = yp.copy()
yp_bad[30] = 40.0
bad_day = sub.index[30]

# A broken axis: the spike is 40%, the rest of the series lives inside +/-6%,
# and drawing both on one scale would flatten everything that matters.
fig, (top, low) = plt.subplots(2, 1, figsize=(11.0, 3.4), sharex=True,
                               gridspec_kw={"height_ratios": [1, 3.2],
                                            "hspace": 0.12})
for ax in (top, low):
    ax.plot(sub.index, ya, color=AMBER, linewidth=1.5, label="actual")
    ax.plot(sub.index, yp_bad, color=BLUE, linewidth=1.5, label="predicted")

top.scatter([bad_day], [40], s=70, color=BRICK, marker="o", zorder=5)
top.annotate("the model says +40%", xy=(bad_day, 40), xytext=(12, -4),
             textcoords="offset points", fontsize=11.5, color=BRICK,
             fontweight="600", va="center")
top.set_ylim(30, 48)
top.set_yticks([40])
top.spines["bottom"].set_visible(False)
top.tick_params(bottom=False)
top.set_title("The last 60 trading days. Actual against predicted", loc="left")

low.set_ylim(-6, 6)
low.spines["top"].set_visible(True)
low.spines["top"].set_color("#c3c2b7")
low.set_ylabel("return (%)")
low.legend(loc="lower left", fontsize=10)

# the diagonal strokes that mark the break, drawn on the axes edges so they
# land in the right place whatever tight_layout decides
break_mark = dict(marker=[(-1, -0.6), (1, 0.6)], markersize=9, linestyle="none",
                  color="#898781", mec="#898781", mew=1.2, clip_on=False)
top.plot([0, 1], [0, 0], transform=top.transAxes, **break_mark)
low.plot([0, 1], [1, 1], transform=low.transAxes, **break_mark)
fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```


# The damage, in numbers

<div class="statband">
<div class="stat"><span class="stat-big">&times;1.8</span><div class="stat-label">MAE, 0.75% to 1.38%</div></div>
<div class="stat stat-warn"><span class="stat-big">&times;5.1</span><div class="stat-label">RMSE, 0.99% to 5.09%</div></div>
<div class="stat stat-warn"><span class="stat-big">&times;26</span><div class="stat-label">MSE</div></div>
</div>

One day in sixty. **MAE says the model got a little worse. MSE says it fell
apart.**

::: {.keypoint}
Neither is lying. They answer different questions: "how wrong is this model on a
normal day" and "how badly can this model hurt me".
:::


# A warning about MAPE

::: {.formula}
$$
\text{MAPE} = \frac{100}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n}\left|\frac{y_i - \hat{y}_i}{y_i}\right|
$$
:::

Percentage error is attractive because it has no units, and it is common in
sales and demand forecasting.

::: {.keypoint}
**Never use it on returns.** A day where the true return is 0.0001 makes the
denominator almost zero, so one ordinary day can contribute thousands of percent
to the average.
:::


# R², and what it compares against

::: {.formula}
$$
R^2 = 1 - \frac{\sum_i (y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2}{\sum_i (y_i - \bar{y})^2}
= 1 - \frac{\text{RSS}}{\text{TSS}}
$$
:::

MAE and RMSE tell you how wrong you were. R² tells you how wrong you were
**compared with the laziest possible model**: always predict the average.

::: {.muted .small}
$R^2 = 0$ means you matched that lazy model. $R^2 = 1$ means you fit every point
exactly.
:::


# The two sums, drawn

```{python}
#| echo: false
#| fig-align: center
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

sub = RET.loc["2024-10":"2024-12"]
xs = sub["SPY"].values * 100
ys = sub["AAPL"].values * 100
pred = (0.00043 + 1.2073 * sub["SPY"].values) * 100
grid = np.linspace(xs.min() - 0.2, xs.max() + 0.2, 40)

fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(11.0, 3.0), sharey=True)
ax = axes[0]
ax.axhline(ys.mean(), color=INK, linewidth=1.8)
ax.vlines(xs, ys.mean(), ys, color=AMBER, linewidth=1.2)
ax.scatter(xs, ys, s=26, color=GREY, zorder=3)
ax.set_title("TSS: distance from the average", loc="left")
ax.set_ylabel("Apple return (%)")
ax.set_xlabel("market return (%)")

ax = axes[1]
ax.plot(grid, 0.043 + 1.2073 * grid, color=INK, linewidth=1.8)
ax.vlines(xs, pred, ys, color=BRICK, linewidth=1.2)
ax.scatter(xs, ys, s=26, color=GREY, zorder=3)
ax.set_title("RSS: distance from the model", loc="left")
ax.set_xlabel("market return (%)")
fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```

R² is one minus the ratio of the red lengths to the amber ones, squared and
added up.


# R² on new data can be negative

```{python}
#| echo: false
#| fig-align: center
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

d = pd.DataFrame({"y": RET["AAPL"], "x": RET["AAPL"].shift(1)}).dropna()
tr, te = d.loc[:"2022"], d.loc["2023":]
Xm = np.column_stack([np.ones(len(tr)), tr["x"]])
coef = np.linalg.lstsq(Xm, tr["y"], rcond=None)[0]

def r2(frame, baseline):
    pred = coef[0] + coef[1] * frame["x"]
    rss = ((frame["y"] - pred) ** 2).sum()
    tss = ((frame["y"] - baseline) ** 2).sum()
    return 1 - rss / tss

vals = [r2(tr, tr["y"].mean()), r2(te, tr["y"].mean())]

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(8.8, 2.3))
ax.bar(["2015 to 2022\n(the data it was fitted on)", "2023 to 2024\n(data it had never seen)"],
       vals, color=[BLUE, BRICK], width=0.45)
ax.axhline(0, color=INK, linewidth=1.2)
ax.set_ylabel("R²")
ax.set_ylim(-0.03, 0.03)
for i, v in enumerate(vals):
    ax.text(i, v + (0.003 if v > 0 else -0.005), f"{v:+.4f}", ha="center",
            fontsize=12, color="#52514e")
ax.set_title("Predicting Apple's return from yesterday's return", loc="left")
fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```

Out of sample there is nothing forcing R² to be positive. **Negative means you
would have done better by always predicting the average.**


# And a tiny R² can still be worth a lot

::: {.keypoint}
An R² of 0.01 on next-day returns would be a serious result. It means 99% of
what happens is noise to you, and the remaining 1%, applied every day across
hundreds of names, is a business.
:::

::: {.muted .small}
This is why finance is unlike most machine learning applications. A vision model
with 1% explained variance is broken. A return model with 1% is a fund. Judge
the number against the problem, never against 1.
:::


# Now change the target

Everything since the start of this part assumed the target was a **number**, so
"how far off were you" had an answer. Change the target to a **label** and that
question stops making sense: what is "up" minus "down"?

<div class="compare">
<div class="compare-col">
<h3>Regression, so far</h3>
<p>predict Apple's return from the market's return</p>
<p>score it by the size of the residuals: MAE, RMSE, R&sup2;</p>
</div>
<div class="compare-col cmp-alt">
<h3>Classification, from here</h3>
<p>predict whether Apple <em>rose</em>, from whether the market rose</p>
<p>there is no residual, so score it by counting right and wrong</p>
</div>
</div>

::: {.muted .small}
Same eleven stocks, same 2,515 days, same market feature. Only the target
column changed, exactly as in Part 2.
:::


# Four things can happen

::: {.why-grid}
::: {.why-point}
**You said up, and it went up** <span>a true positive</span>
:::
::: {.why-point}
**You said up, and it went down** <span>a false positive, a false alarm</span>
:::
::: {.why-point}
**You said down, and it went up** <span>a false negative, a miss</span>
:::
::: {.why-point}
**You said down, and it went down** <span>a true negative</span>
:::
:::

Every classification metric in use is built out of those four counts, and
nothing else.


# The confusion matrix

::: {.diagram}
<svg width="1000" height="300" viewBox="0 0 1000 300" role="img" aria-label="Confusion matrix for predicting whether Apple rose from whether the market rose: 1042 true positives, 297 false negatives, 331 false positives, 845 true negatives">
  <text x="560" y="34" text-anchor="middle" font-size="15" font-family="Segoe UI, sans-serif" fill="#898781" letter-spacing="1.5">WHAT THE MODEL SAID</text>
  <text x="430" y="70" text-anchor="middle" font-size="16" font-family="Segoe UI, sans-serif" fill="#0d366b" font-weight="600">up</text>
  <text x="690" y="70" text-anchor="middle" font-size="16" font-family="Segoe UI, sans-serif" fill="#0d366b" font-weight="600">down</text>
  <text x="285" y="140" text-anchor="end" font-size="16" font-family="Segoe UI, sans-serif" fill="#0d366b" font-weight="600">it went up</text>
  <text x="285" y="230" text-anchor="end" font-size="16" font-family="Segoe UI, sans-serif" fill="#0d366b" font-weight="600">it went down</text>
  <text x="285" y="70" text-anchor="end" font-size="15" font-family="Segoe UI, sans-serif" fill="#898781" letter-spacing="1.5">WHAT HAPPENED</text>
  <rect x="300" y="88" width="260" height="90" rx="6" fill="#eef4fc" stroke="#1c5cab" stroke-width="1.5"/>
  <rect x="560" y="88" width="260" height="90" rx="6" fill="#fbf1f0" stroke="#e0b3ac" stroke-width="1.5"/>
  <rect x="300" y="178" width="260" height="90" rx="6" fill="#fbf1f0" stroke="#e0b3ac" stroke-width="1.5"/>
  <rect x="560" y="178" width="260" height="90" rx="6" fill="#eef4fc" stroke="#1c5cab" stroke-width="1.5"/>
  <g font-family="Segoe UI, sans-serif" text-anchor="middle">
    <text x="430" y="126" font-size="26" font-weight="700" fill="#0d366b">1042</text>
    <text x="430" y="152" font-size="13.5" fill="#52514e">true positives</text>
    <text x="690" y="126" font-size="26" font-weight="700" fill="#b3402f">297</text>
    <text x="690" y="152" font-size="13.5" fill="#8a4038">false negatives (misses)</text>
    <text x="430" y="216" font-size="26" font-weight="700" fill="#b3402f">331</text>
    <text x="430" y="242" font-size="13.5" fill="#8a4038">false positives (false alarms)</text>
    <text x="690" y="216" font-size="26" font-weight="700" fill="#0d366b">845</text>
    <text x="690" y="242" font-size="13.5" fill="#52514e">true negatives</text>
  </g>
</svg>
:::

::: {.diagram-note}
Predicting "Apple rose today" from "the market rose today", over 2,515 days.
:::


# Counting them yourself

```{pyodide}
said_up = market > 0
went_up = actual > 0

tp = (said_up & went_up).sum()
fp = (said_up & ~went_up).sum()
fn = (~said_up & went_up).sum()
tn = (~said_up & ~went_up).sum()

print("TP %d   FP %d   FN %d   TN %d" % (tp, fp, fn, tn))
print("accuracy: %.4f" % ((tp + tn) / (tp + fp + fn + tn)))
```

::: {.muted .small}
Four boolean masks and four sums. Nothing here that Session 3 did not already
give you.
:::


# Accuracy

::: {.formula}
$$
\text{accuracy} = \frac{TP + TN}{TP + TN + FP + FN}
$$
:::

**75.0% of days called correctly.** It is the first number everybody computes,
and on its own it is close to useless.


# Where accuracy falls apart

Now predict something rare: a day when Apple falls more than 5%.

<div class="statband">
<div class="stat"><span class="stat-big">22</span><div class="stat-label">such days in ten years</div></div>
<div class="stat"><span class="stat-big">99.1%</span><div class="stat-label">accuracy of a model that always says "no crash"</div></div>
<div class="stat stat-warn"><span class="stat-big">0</span><div class="stat-label">crashes it would ever warn you about</div></div>
</div>

::: {.keypoint}
A model with no skill whatsoever beats almost anything you will build, measured
by accuracy. **When one class is rare, accuracy measures the class balance, not
the model.**
:::


# Precision and recall

::: {.formula}
$$
\text{precision} = \frac{TP}{TP + FP}
\qquad
\text{recall} = \frac{TP}{TP + FN}
$$
:::

<div class="compare">
<div class="compare-col">
<h3>Precision: 75.9%</h3>
<p>Of the days I called up, how many really were?</p>
<p>Low precision means <strong>false alarms</strong>: you traded on a signal that was not there.</p>
</div>
<div class="compare-col cmp-alt">
<h3>Recall: 77.8%</h3>
<p>Of the days that really were up, how many did I catch?</p>
<p>Low recall means <strong>misses</strong>: the move happened and you were not in it.</p>
</div>
</div>

::: {.muted .small}
Neither uses the true negatives, which is exactly why they survive an imbalanced
problem when accuracy does not.
:::


# You choose where to sit

```{python}
#| echo: false
#| fig-align: center
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

score = 0.00043 + 1.2073 * RET["SPY"].values
truth = RET["AAPL"].values > 0
ths = np.quantile(score, np.linspace(0.002, 0.998, 250))
prec, rec = [], []
for t in ths:
    pred = score >= t
    tp = (pred & truth).sum()
    fp = (pred & ~truth).sum()
    fn = (~pred & truth).sum()
    prec.append(tp / max(tp + fp, 1))
    rec.append(tp / max(tp + fn, 1))

fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(11.0, 3.0))
ax = axes[0]
ax.hist(score[truth] * 100, bins=60, color=BLUE, alpha=0.65, label="really up")
ax.hist(score[~truth] * 100, bins=60, color=BRICK, alpha=0.55, label="really down")
ax.axvline(0, color=INK, linewidth=1.8)
ax.text(0.15, ax.get_ylim()[1] * 0.82, "threshold", fontsize=10.5, color=INK)
ax.set_xlabel("model score (%)")
ax.set_ylabel("days")
ax.set_title("Move the line, change the trade-off", loc="left")
ax.legend(loc="upper left", fontsize=9.5)

ax = axes[1]
ax.plot(ths * 100, prec, color=BLUE, linewidth=2.2, label="precision")
ax.plot(ths * 100, rec, color=AMBER, linewidth=2.2, label="recall")
ax.set_xlabel("threshold (%)")
ax.set_ylabel("rate")
ax.set_title("One goes up as the other goes down", loc="left")
ax.legend(loc="center right", fontsize=9.5)
fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```

Demand a score above +1% before calling a day up, and precision climbs to
**91%** while recall collapses to **30%**.


# One number, if you must have one

::: {.formula}
$$
F_1 = 2 \cdot \frac{\text{precision} \cdot \text{recall}}{\text{precision} + \text{recall}}
$$
:::

The harmonic mean, which is close to the smaller of the two. **Our model scores
0.768.**

::: {.muted .small}
A plain average would let 100% precision and 1% recall look respectable. The
harmonic mean does not: it lands at 2%.
:::


# The whole trade-off, in one curve

```{python}
#| echo: false
#| fig-align: center
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

score = 0.00043 + 1.2073 * RET["SPY"].values
truth = RET["AAPL"].values > 0
order = np.argsort(-score)
t_sorted = truth[order]
tpr = np.cumsum(t_sorted) / t_sorted.sum()
fpr = np.cumsum(~t_sorted) / (~t_sorted).sum()
auc = np.sum(np.diff(fpr) * (tpr[1:] + tpr[:-1]) / 2)   # trapezoid rule

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(6.6, 3.2))
ax.plot([0, 1], [0, 1], color=GREY, linewidth=1.4, linestyle="--",
        label="a coin flip")
ax.plot(fpr, tpr, color=BLUE, linewidth=2.4, label=f"the model (AUC {auc:.2f})")
ax.set_xlabel("false positive rate")
ax.set_ylabel("true positive rate (recall)")
ax.set_title("ROC curve", loc="left")
ax.legend(loc="lower right")
fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```

Every threshold is one point on that curve. **AUC** is the area under it: 0.5 is
a coin flip, 1.0 is perfect.


# Which metric, and who decides

| the question | the metric | why |
|---|---|---|
| how far off is a typical forecast | MAE | in the units of the target, robust |
| how badly can it hurt me | RMSE | large errors dominate, which is the point |
| is it better than predicting the mean | R² | scale free, comparable across targets |
| the target is a label, classes balanced | accuracy | simple and honest here |
| the target is rare | precision and recall | accuracy is meaningless |
| I need to compare across thresholds | ROC AUC | threshold free |

: {.vocab}

::: {.keypoint}
The metric is a business decision, not a technical one. What does a false alarm
cost, and what does a miss cost?
:::


# Your turn [4]{.chip .chip-turn} {.your-turn}

A "crash warning" model says the day will be bad whenever the market itself is
down more than 1%. Compute its **recall** on Apple's 5% down days.

```{pyodide}
#| autorun: false
crash = actual < -0.05
warned = market < -0.01

hits = ...
recall = ...

print("crash days:", crash.sum())
print("recall    :", recall)
```


# Your turn 4: answer

```python
crash = actual < -0.05
warned = market < -0.01

hits = (warned & crash).sum()
recall = hits / crash.sum()

print("crash days:", crash.sum())
print("recall    :", recall)
```

**It catches 19 of the 22 crashes, a recall of 0.864.** Before you celebrate,
ask the other question: it warns on 278 days in total, so its precision is
0.068. Thirteen false alarms for every real one.


# 5 · Overfitting, and the trade-off {.divider}

::: {.divider-kicker}
Part 5
:::

::: {.divider-note}
Why the model that fits best is usually the one to throw away.
:::


# A model that is never wrong

```{python}
#| echo: false
#| fig-align: center
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from numpy.polynomial import Polynomial

fit15 = Polynomial.fit(XT, YT, 15)
mse15 = ((fit15(XT) - YT) ** 2).mean()

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(10.4, 3.2))
ax.plot(XGRID, fit15(XGRID), color=BLUE, linewidth=2.2)
ax.scatter(XT, YT, s=44, color=AMBER, zorder=3)
ax.set_ylim(-2.2, 2.2)
ax.set_xlabel("feature $x$")
ax.set_ylabel("target $y$")
ax.set_title(f"Training MSE = {mse15:.3f}", loc="left")
fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```

```{pyodide}
#| include: false
# The invented data from Part 1, put into the live namespace so the dial cell a
# few slides on stays short enough to read on a slide.
rng = np.random.default_rng(11)
x_train = np.sort(rng.uniform(0, 1, 30))
y_train = np.sin(2 * np.pi * x_train) + rng.normal(0, 0.28, 30)
grid = np.linspace(0, 1, 200)
```

The same invented data from Part 1, and a curve that passes through almost every
point. **By every metric in Part 4, this is an excellent model.**


# So: is that the model you want? {.your-turn}

Before the next slide, decide what you think.

- What does this curve say will happen at $x = 0.55$?
- If I collected thirty new observations, would it still fit them?
- Which parts of that curve are the relationship, and which are the noise?

::: {.muted .small}
There is no trick. The answer is the whole of the rest of this session.
:::


# Three fits of the same thirty points

```{python}
#| echo: false
#| fig-align: center
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from numpy.polynomial import Polynomial

fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 3, figsize=(11.0, 2.9), sharey=True)
for ax, deg in zip(axes, [1, 4, 15]):
    p = Polynomial.fit(XT, YT, deg)
    ax.plot(XGRID, true_f(XGRID), color=GREY, linewidth=1.4, linestyle="--")
    ax.plot(XGRID, p(XGRID), color=BLUE, linewidth=2.2)
    ax.scatter(XT, YT, s=26, color=AMBER, zorder=3)
    ax.set_ylim(-2.2, 2.2)
    ax.set_xlabel("$x$")
    ax.set_title(f"degree {deg}: train MSE {((p(XT) - YT) ** 2).mean():.3f}",
                 loc="left", fontsize=12)
axes[0].set_ylabel("$y$")
fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```

Grey dashed is the truth. **Training error falls every time you add flexibility,
and it will keep falling all the way to zero.**


# The same three, on data they never saw

```{python}
#| echo: false
#| fig-align: center
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from numpy.polynomial import Polynomial

fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 3, figsize=(11.0, 2.9), sharey=True)
for ax, deg in zip(axes, [1, 4, 15]):
    p = Polynomial.fit(XT, YT, deg)
    ax.plot(XGRID, true_f(XGRID), color=GREY, linewidth=1.4, linestyle="--")
    ax.plot(XGRID, p(XGRID), color=BLUE, linewidth=2.2)
    ax.scatter(XE, YE, s=8, color=BRICK, alpha=0.35, linewidths=0, zorder=3)
    ax.set_ylim(-2.2, 2.2)
    ax.set_xlabel("$x$")
    ax.set_title(f"degree {deg}: test MSE {((p(XE) - YE) ** 2).mean():.3f}",
                 loc="left", fontsize=12)
axes[0].set_ylabel("$y$")
fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```

Four hundred new points. The middle model is now the best by a distance, and the
flexible one is **thirty times worse** than it was on its own training data.


# Two ways to be wrong

<div class="compare">
<div class="compare-col">
<h3>Underfitting</h3>
<p>The model is too rigid to represent the relationship.</p>
<p>Bad on the training data, bad on new data, and <strong>bad in the same way on both</strong>.</p>
<p>The straight line above.</p>
</div>
<div class="compare-col cmp-alt">
<h3>Overfitting</h3>
<p>The model has learned the noise as if it were signal.</p>
<p>Excellent on the training data, poor on new data. <strong>The gap is the symptom.</strong></p>
<p>The degree-15 curve above.</p>
</div>
</div>

::: {.keypoint}
You cannot tell them apart from the training score. That is the entire reason
for everything on the next few slides.
:::


# The picture worth remembering

```{python}
#| echo: false
#| fig-align: center
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from numpy.polynomial import Polynomial

degrees = np.arange(1, 16)
tr_err, te_err = [], []
for d in degrees:
    p = Polynomial.fit(XT, YT, d)
    tr_err.append(((p(XT) - YT) ** 2).mean())
    te_err.append(((p(XE) - YE) ** 2).mean())
best = degrees[int(np.argmin(te_err))]

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(10.4, 3.3))
ax.plot(degrees, tr_err, color=BLUE, linewidth=2.4, marker="o", markersize=4,
        label="training error")
ax.plot(degrees, te_err, color=BRICK, linewidth=2.4, marker="o", markersize=4,
        label="test error")
ax.axhline(SD ** 2, color=GREY, linewidth=1.4, linestyle="--")
ax.text(14.6, SD ** 2 + 0.02, "irreducible error", ha="right", fontsize=10.5,
        color="#898781")
ax.axvline(best, color=AMBER, linewidth=1.4)
ax.text(best + 0.25, 0.028, "the model you want", fontsize=11, color="#8a6508")
ax.set_yscale("log")
ax.set_xlabel("flexibility (polynomial degree)")
ax.set_ylabel("mean squared error (log scale)")
ax.set_xticks(degrees)
ax.legend(loc="upper center")
fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```

::: {.muted .small}
Training error falls forever. Test error falls, bottoms out, and then climbs.
Every model selection procedure in this course is a way of finding that bottom
without cheating.
:::


# Turn the dial yourself

```{pyodide}
#| autorun: false
degree = 3                          # try 1, then 4, then 15
coef = np.polyfit(x_train, y_train, degree)

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(8.0, 1.5))
ax.scatter(x_train, y_train, s=26, color="#b8860b", zorder=3)
ax.plot(grid, np.polyval(coef, grid), color="#1c5cab", linewidth=2.2)
ax.set_ylim(-2.4, 2.4)
plt.show()
```

::: {.muted .small}
`x_train` and `y_train` are the thirty invented points from a few slides back.
:::


# Which is why you held rows back

Training error kept improving all the way to degree 15. Test error stopped
improving at 5. **Only one of those two numbers was available while the curve
was being chosen.**

::: {.keypoint}
The rows you set aside in Part 1 are what turn "my model fits well" into
evidence. Everything after today, cross-validation included, is a way of
estimating that test error better without spending the test set.
:::


# Splitting the table

```{pyodide}
train = aapl.loc[:"2022-12-31"]
test = aapl.loc["2023-01-01":]
```
