```{pyodide}
print("train rows:", len(train), "from", train.index.min().date(),
      "to", train.index.max().date())
print("test rows :", len(test), "from", test.index.min().date(),
      "to", test.index.max().date())
```

::: {.muted .small}
`aapl` is the feature table from Part 1. Splitting it is one line, because a
DataFrame index knows about dates.
:::


# Time series break the usual rule

```{python}
#| echo: false
#| fig-align: center
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

n = 120
rs = np.random.default_rng(4)
is_test_random = rs.random(n) < 0.25
is_test_chrono = np.arange(n) >= int(n * 0.75)

fig, axes = plt.subplots(2, 1, figsize=(11.0, 2.7), sharex=True)
for ax, flags, ttl in [
    (axes[0], is_test_random, "Shuffled: test days scattered through the sample"),
    (axes[1], is_test_chrono, "Chronological: test days are the future"),
]:
    ax.bar(np.arange(n), np.ones(n), width=1.0,
           color=[AMBER if f else BLUE for f in flags])
    ax.set_yticks([])
    ax.grid(False)
    ax.set_title(ttl, loc="left", fontsize=12)
    for side in ("left", "bottom"):
        ax.spines[side].set_visible(False)
axes[1].set_xlabel("trading day")
fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```

In the top panel the model trains on Wednesday and Friday to predict Thursday.
**It knows the future.** Any backtest built that way will look wonderful and
lose money.


# The many faces of look-ahead bias

::: {.why-grid}
::: {.why-point}
**Shuffling a time series** <span>the split has to be a date, not a random draw</span>
:::
::: {.why-point}
**Scaling on the full sample** <span>the mean you subtracted contains the test period</span>
:::
::: {.why-point}
**Interpolating across a gap** <span>Part 3: the fill used the price after the gap</span>
:::
::: {.why-point}
**Choosing features after peeking** <span>you tried forty ideas and kept the one the test set liked</span>
:::
::: {.why-point}
**Restated fundamentals** <span>the numbers in the database today are not the ones published then</span>
:::
::: {.why-point}
**Today's index members** <span>the failures dropped out, so the sample is only the survivors</span>
:::
:::


# Where the error comes from

Test error is not one thing. Split it into two, and everything about model
choice becomes clearer.

<div class="compare">
<div class="compare-col">
<h3>Bias</h3>
<p>Error from the model being too simple to represent the truth.</p>
<p>It would still be there if you had a million rows.</p>
</div>
<div class="compare-col cmp-alt">
<h3>Variance</h3>
<p>Error from the model being too sensitive to the particular sample you drew.</p>
<p>Collect a different sample, get a different model.</p>
</div>
</div>


# The same model, twenty different samples

```{python}
#| echo: false
#| fig-align: center
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from numpy.polynomial import Polynomial

fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(11.0, 3.0), sharey=True)
for ax, deg, ttl in [(axes[0], 1, "Degree 1: high bias, low variance"),
                     (axes[1], 12, "Degree 12: low bias, high variance")]:
    for k in range(20):
        g = np.random.default_rng(200 + k)
        xs = np.sort(g.uniform(0, 1, 30))
        ys = true_f(xs) + g.normal(0, SD, 30)
        ax.plot(XGRID, Polynomial.fit(xs, ys, deg)(XGRID), color=BLUE_LIGHT,
                linewidth=0.9, alpha=0.75)
    ax.plot(XGRID, true_f(XGRID), color=INK, linewidth=2.2)
    ax.set_ylim(-2.6, 2.6)
    ax.set_xlabel("$x$")
    ax.set_title(ttl, loc="left", fontsize=12)
axes[0].set_ylabel("$y$")
fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```

Left: every line is wrong in the same way, and they all miss the curve. Right:
the lines are right on average and **wildly different from each other**.


# Both, on one target

```{python}
#| echo: false
#| fig-align: center
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

cases = [("Low bias, low variance", 0.00, 0.10),
         ("Low bias, high variance", 0.00, 0.42),
         ("High bias, low variance", 0.62, 0.10),
         ("High bias, high variance", 0.62, 0.42)]

fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 4, figsize=(11.0, 3.0))
for ax, (ttl, off, spread) in zip(axes, cases):
    for radius, colour in [(1.0, "#f0eee6"), (0.66, "#e3e9f2"), (0.33, "#cfdff2")]:
        ax.add_patch(plt.Circle((0, 0), radius, color=colour, zorder=1))
    ax.add_patch(plt.Circle((0, 0), 0.1, color=BRICK, zorder=2))
    g = np.random.default_rng(9)
    ang = g.uniform(0, 2 * np.pi)
    cx, cy = off * np.cos(ang), off * np.sin(ang)
    ax.scatter(cx + g.normal(0, spread, 14), cy + g.normal(0, spread, 14),
               s=26, color=BLUE, zorder=3)
    ax.set_xlim(-1.15, 1.15)
    ax.set_ylim(-1.15, 1.15)
    ax.set_aspect("equal")
    ax.set_xticks([])
    ax.set_yticks([])
    ax.grid(False)
    for side in ("left", "bottom"):
        ax.spines[side].set_visible(False)
    ax.set_title(ttl, fontsize=11.5)
fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```

Each dot is the model you would have got from one sample. **Bias is how far the
cluster sits from the centre. Variance is how spread out it is.**


# The decomposition

::: {.formula}
$$
\mathbb{E}\left[\left(y_0 - \hat{f}(x_0)\right)^2\right] =
\underbrace{\operatorname{Var}\!\left(\hat{f}(x_0)\right)}_{\text{variance}} +
\underbrace{\left[\operatorname{Bias}\!\left(\hat{f}(x_0)\right)\right]^2}_{\text{bias}^2} +
\underbrace{\operatorname{Var}(\varepsilon)}_{\text{irreducible}}
$$
:::

Three non-negative terms. Expected test error can never fall below the third
one, and **making one of the first two smaller usually makes the other larger.**

::: {.muted .small}
This is a statement about expected error over all possible training samples, not
about the one sample you happen to have.
:::


# The trade-off, drawn

```{python}
#| echo: false
#| fig-align: center
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from numpy.polynomial import Polynomial

xs_eval = np.linspace(0.03, 0.97, 80)
truth_eval = true_f(xs_eval)

def knn_predict(query, x, y, k):
    return np.array([y[np.argsort(np.abs(x - q))[:k]].mean() for q in query])

neighbours = [40, 25, 15, 10, 7, 5, 3, 2, 1]
bias2, var = [], []
for k in neighbours:
    preds = []
    for s in range(250):
        g = np.random.default_rng(9000 + s)
        xs = np.sort(g.uniform(0, 1, 60))
        ys = true_f(xs) + g.normal(0, SD, 60)
        preds.append(knn_predict(xs_eval, xs, ys, k))
    A = np.array(preds)
    bias2.append(((A.mean(axis=0) - truth_eval) ** 2).mean())
    var.append(A.var(axis=0).mean())
bias2, var = np.array(bias2), np.array(var)
total = bias2 + var + SD ** 2
pos = np.arange(len(neighbours))

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(10.4, 3.3))
ax.plot(pos, bias2, color=AMBER, linewidth=2.4, marker="o", markersize=4,
        label="bias$^2$")
ax.plot(pos, var, color=BLUE, linewidth=2.4, marker="o", markersize=4,
        label="variance")
ax.plot(pos, total, color=BRICK, linewidth=2.6, marker="o", markersize=4,
        label="expected test error")
ax.axhline(SD ** 2, color=GREY, linewidth=1.4, linestyle="--")
ax.text(len(pos) - 1, SD ** 2 + 0.008, "irreducible", ha="right", fontsize=10.5,
        color="#898781")
ax.set_xticks(pos)
ax.set_xticklabels(neighbours)
ax.set_xlabel("neighbours averaged  (fewer to the right, so more flexible)")
ax.set_ylabel("contribution to error")
ax.set_ylim(0, 0.32)
ax.legend(loc="upper center", ncol=3)
fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```

Bias falls, variance rises, and their sum has a minimum somewhere in between.
**The red curve is the same U as before, now explained.**

::: {.muted .small}
The dial here is the number of neighbours rather than a polynomial degree, since
it separates the two pieces more cleanly. Flexibility is the general idea; the
particular knob does not matter.
:::


# What you actually do about it

::: {.why-grid}
::: {.why-point}
**Get more data** <span>variance falls as $n$ grows, and nothing else is as reliable</span>
:::
::: {.why-point}
**Use fewer features** <span>every extra column is another way to fit noise</span>
:::
::: {.why-point}
**Simplify the model** <span>a linear model is hard to overfit, which is why it is still the benchmark</span>
:::
::: {.why-point}
**Penalise complexity** <span>ridge and lasso deliberately add bias to remove more variance</span>
:::
::: {.why-point}
**Cross-validate** <span>reuse the training data to estimate test error, without touching the test set</span>
:::
::: {.why-point}
**Hold something back** <span>and do not look at it until you have finished deciding</span>
:::
:::

::: {.muted .small}
The last three are the subject of the next block. You now know what problem they
are solving.
:::


# There is no best model

::: {.keypoint}
There is no model that is best for every problem. Whether flexibility helps
depends on the shape of the true $f$ and on how much noise sits on top of it,
and you do not get to see either one.
:::

So the job is not to find the best model. **It is to make the trade-off
deliberately, and to be honest about how you measured it.**


# What stays with you {.smaller}

::: {.recap-grid}
::: {.recap-item}
**One table**
Rows are observations, columns are features, one column is the target.
:::
::: {.recap-item}
**$Y = f(X) + \varepsilon$**
You estimate $f$. You never remove $\varepsilon$.
:::
::: {.recap-item}
**Supervised or not**
A target column decides it. Number means regression, label means classification.
:::
::: {.recap-item}
**Cleaning is the work**
Missing, extreme, wrong scale, not numeric. In that order, most days.
:::
::: {.recap-item}
**Nothing from the future**
Not in a fill, not in a scaler, not in a split.
:::
::: {.recap-item}
**Pick the metric first**
MAE, RMSE and R² answer different questions. So do precision and recall.
:::
::: {.recap-item}
**Training error always falls**
Which is exactly why it cannot be used to choose a model.
:::
::: {.recap-item}
**Bias against variance**
Too simple misses the shape. Too flexible learns the noise.
:::
:::


# Where this goes next

<div class="timeline">
<div class="tl-row tl-now"><span class="tl-week">now</span><span class="tl-topic">the framework, the metrics, the trade-off</span><span class="tl-who">no models fitted</span></div>
<div class="tl-row"><span class="tl-week">next</span><span class="tl-topic">model selection and cross-validation</span><span class="tl-who">finding the bottom of the U</span></div>
<div class="tl-row tl-future"><span class="tl-week">then</span><span class="tl-topic">regression and classification methods, trees, ensembles</span><span class="tl-who">scikit-learn</span></div>
</div>

::: {.keypoint}
Everything from here is a different way of building $\hat{f}$. The questions you
ask of it are the ones from today, and they do not change.
:::


# The course case, Part 4 [case]{.chip .chip-case}

Four sessions ago the case asked which of two stocks was riskier. It has grown
every session since:

::: {.why-grid}
::: {.why-point}
**Part 1** <span>two stocks, a first look, arithmetic by hand</span>
:::
::: {.why-point}
**Part 2** <span>proper volatility, with loops and a function</span>
:::
::: {.why-point}
**Part 3** <span>all eleven stocks, pandas, and figures</span>
:::
::: {.why-point}
**Part 4** <span>the same data, reframed as a prediction problem</span>
:::
:::


# Case Part 4: this week

You will take the table you built in Part 3 and turn it into a learning problem:

- choose a target, and build the features that are known before it
- find the missing values and the extremes, and decide what to do about each
- split by date, and say why the split has to be a date
- choose the metric you would judge a model by, and defend the choice
- state the prediction question precisely enough for somebody to answer it

::: {.keypoint}
You still do not fit anything. Part 4 finishes the setup, so that the first
model you ever fit is one you already know how to judge.
:::


# Before next time

- **Exercises**: the statistical learning framework, data problems, metrics, and
  over- and underfitting. Longer than usual, and worth the time.
- **Case Part 4**: the risk report becomes a prediction problem.

::: {.muted .small}
Stuck? Ask a friend, ask an AI for a hint rather than an answer, or email me at
**jobo@econ.au.dk**.
:::
